era_context: The Roman Empire at the end of the Flavian dynasty; Emperor Domitian is assassinated in September 96 CE and is succeeded by Nerva, marking the beginning of the Nerva-Antonine dynasty. In China, the Eastern Han dynasty under Emperor He is in the early years of his reign (r. 88–105 CE). This is a pivotal year marking major political transitions in both empires.

events.title: Assassination of Emperor Domitian and accession of Nerva
events.description: Emperor Domitian is assassinated on September 18, 96 CE, after a reign of 15 years (81–96 CE). He is killed in a conspiracy involving members of the Senate, the Praetorian Guard, and his own household. Domitian is succeeded by Marcus Cocceius Nerva, a respected senator who is proclaimed emperor by the Senate. Nerva's accession marks the end of the Flavian dynasty and the beginning of the Nerva-Antonine dynasty, which will rule Rome for nearly a century. Domitian's assassination is celebrated by the Senate and the upper classes, who had suffered under his autocratic rule.

sources.name: Suetonius, Lives of the Caesars (Domitian and Nerva)

events.certainty_note: Multiple sources confirm Domitian's assassination on September 18, 96 CE, and Nerva's accession. The date is well-established.

events.title: Nerva's early reforms and restoration of Senate authority
events.description: Following his accession, Emperor Nerva implements a series of reforms intended to restore the authority of the Senate and address grievances against Domitian's rule. He cancels Domitian's persecutions, restores confiscated property, and attempts to establish a more consultative relationship with the Senate. However, Nerva's authority is quickly challenged by the Praetorian Guard and the military, who feel that their interests are not being adequately represented.
events.certainty_note: Nerva's reforms are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources, particularly Pliny's Panegyricus.

events.title: Emperor He of Han's reign continues
events.description: Emperor He of Han (r. 88–105 CE) continues his reign in 96 CE. The emperor is approximately 17 years old and his authority is constrained by regents and powerful court factions. The Han court maintains control over the empire's vast territories and continues to manage the Silk Road trade networks.

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han)

events.certainty_note: Emperor He's reign is well-documented in Chinese sources; his continued rule in 96 CE is confirmed.

events.title: Pliny the Younger's governorship and administrative activities
events.description: Pliny the Younger, a prominent senator and author, is active in Roman politics and administration during this period. He is known for his correspondence with Emperor Trajan (later) and his literary works. In 96 CE, Pliny is likely involved in the political transitions following Domitian's assassination and is a supporter of Nerva's reforms.
key_figures: Pliny the Younger (Roman senator and author)

sources.name: Pliny the Younger, Letters and Panegyricus

events.certainty_note: Pliny's activities in 96 CE are not uniquely documented, but his involvement in the political transitions of this period is likely based on his known career trajectory.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, some modern scholars debate the exact date of Domitian's assassination; while September 18, 96 CE is the traditional date, some sources suggest it may have occurred on a different date in September. The consensus, however, supports September 18, 96 CE. Additionally, the extent of Nerva's reforms and their effectiveness is debated by modern scholars; some argue that Nerva's authority was more limited than traditional sources suggest.
historiographic_note: The year 96 CE is very well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly the assassination of Domitian and the accession of Nerva. Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources provide detailed accounts. Pliny the Younger's Panegyricus, delivered in 100 CE, provides a contemporary perspective on these events. For China, the record is less detailed for this specific year, relying on later compilations. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite political events and military campaigns; the lives of ordinary people are largely invisible. Roman sources reflect the perspectives of the Senate and the upper classes, who benefited from Domitian's assassination. Chinese sources are similarly elite-focused and compiled centuries after the events.

graph_edges.note: Domitian's assassination leads to Nerva's reforms
Domitian's assassination and Nerva's accession lead to the adoption of Trajan
Nerva's reforms and the challenges to his authority lead to the adoption of Trajan
